questions.question: Which attribute on the <script> tag specifies the URL of an external JavaScript file?
questions.explanation: The src attribute on the

questions.question: What does the defer attribute do on a <script> tag?
choices: Prevents the script from running entirely
Downloads the script in parallel and executes it after HTML parsing is complete
Executes the script before any HTML is parsed
Loads the script only when the user clicks a button
questions.explanation: The defer attribute downloads the script in parallel with HTML parsing but delays execution until the HTML document has been fully parsed.

questions.question: What is the key difference between async and defer on a script tag?
choices: async only works with inline scripts
async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits until HTML parsing is complete
questions.explanation: Both async and defer download scripts in parallel, but async executes immediately upon download (potentially interrupting parsing), while defer waits until parsing finishes.

questions.question: Where is the recommended placement for <script> tags that don't use async or defer?
questions.explanation: Placing scripts just before </body> ensures the HTML content loads first, preventing the script from blocking page rendering.

questions.question: The ___ attribute on a script tag causes it to download in parallel and execute immediately when ready.
questions.explanation: The async attribute downloads the script without blocking HTML parsing and executes it as soon as the download completes, regardless of parsing state.

questions.question: A <script> tag with JavaScript written directly between the opening and closing tags is called an ___ script.
questions.explanation: Inline scripts contain JavaScript code directly in the HTML file, as opposed to external scripts which reference a separate .js file via the src attribute.
